title: The Great Heist at Musée des Lumières
subtitle: Paris, 2026
description: During the annual Nuit d'Or gala at Paris's most prestigious museum, Caravaggio's lost masterpiece Le Dernier Souffle was stolen. The painting, valued at 200 million Euros, was removed from a biometric vault and replaced with a flawless forgery. Security cameras were looped for 11 minutes. Night security chief Lucien Moreau was arrested, but the real thieves are still at large. 8 suspects, 2 secret accomplices, and one innocent prisoner. Can you unravel Paris's darkest secret?

characters.name: Marguerite Delacroix
characters.relationToVictim: The museum's top executive, one of only 3 people with vault access authorization
characters.alibi: I was on stage throughout the gala. I gave speeches and attended to the guests. I have hundreds of witnesses.

characters.name: Rémi Vasseur
characters.relationToVictim: Has direct access to the paintings, restoration workshop is near the vault
characters.alibi: On the night of the gala, I was working in the restoration workshop. I was cleaning a Renoir painting. I was alone.

characters.name: Isabelle Chen
characters.relationToVictim: Oversees the museum's insurance policy, has copies of the security plans
characters.alibi: I was conducting a routine inspection on the floor where the vault is located. I do it every year on behalf of the insurance company.

characters.name: Antoine Beaumont
characters.relationToVictim: Has been trying to buy the painting for 20 years, gala invitee
characters.alibi: I was drinking champagne at the gala. I was in the bar area. The waiters will remember me.

characters.name: Nadia Sokolov
characters.relationToVictim: Museum intern with access to the restoration lab, trained in reproduction at the Paris School of Fine Arts
characters.alibi: I was on duty at the gala, helping the guests. I went to the restroom at one point, that's all.

characters.name: Jean-Pierre Dumas
characters.relationToVictim: Personally designed the museum's security system, fired 6 months ago
characters.alibi: I didn't even go near the museum that night. I was sitting at the cafe across the street, drinking beer.

characters.name: Camille Laurent
characters.relationToVictim: Investigated twice for black market art sales, gala invitee
characters.alibi: I was networking at the gala. I spoke with dozens of people. I didn't even go near the vault.

characters.name: Hugo Marchand
characters.relationToVictim: Published an article questioning the painting's authenticity 2 weeks before the heist
characters.alibi: I was wandering around the gala with my press badge. I was taking photos and conducting interviews.

characters.name: Lucien Moreau
characters.relationToVictim: Security guard with vault access card, the first person arrested in the heist
characters.alibi: I was on guard duty on the vault floor during the gala night. My card was cloned, I didn't do it.

evidence.title: Camera Loop Recording
evidence.description: System log showing the security cameras were looped for 11 minutes. The loop was triggered from one of 4 terminals on the internal network.

evidence.title: Forged Painting Analysis
evidence.description: Forensic report confirming the Le Dernier Souffle from the vault is a forgery. The forger is an expert in 17th-century techniques and spent at least 6 months in preparation.

evidence.title: Restoration Reports
evidence.description: Strange technical notes found in Rémi's restoration reports: 'Pigment 7 ready', 'Frame B compatible'. Normal academic terminology or coded communication?

evidence.title: Biometric Vault Records
evidence.description: Access log showing the vault remained open for 7 minutes. Lucien's fingerprint was detected, but he was in the security room at the time.

evidence.title: Gala Entry-Exit Records
evidence.description: Records showing 6 suspects were in suspicious locations during the critical 11 minutes of the Nuit d'Or gala. Everyone has an explanation, but none are fully convincing.

evidence.title: Lucien's Cloned Card
evidence.description: Technical analysis showing that Lucien Moreau's security card was professionally cloned. This finding is strong evidence of Lucien's innocence.

evidence.title: Lucien's Bank Account
evidence.description: Bank records showing that Lucien's suspicious-looking account transactions actually stem from a documented house sale. Supports the claim that Lucien had no financial motive for the heist.

evidence.title: Hidden Service Corridor
evidence.description: A hidden service corridor providing direct passage from the restoration workshop to the biometric vault. Paint residue was found on the floor, and multiple people knew of this corridor's existence.

evidence.title: Philippe's 11-Minute Gap
evidence.description: It was discovered that security guard Philippe left his post during the critical 11 minutes. It came to light that he was paid 500 Euros by someone to leave the corridor unguarded.

evidence.title: Camille's Encrypted Phone
evidence.description: It was discovered that Camille uses a second encrypted phone and made 3 international calls on the night of the gala. The time zones don't match, and Camille refuses to explain.

evidence.title: Antoine's Secret Offer
evidence.description: A hidden room was found in Antoine's home along with 8 months of correspondence with replica artist Mehmet Kaya in Istanbul. He ordered an exact copy of Le Dernier Souffle, delivered 2 weeks before the gala.

evidence.title: Hugo's Unpublished Article
evidence.description: It was revealed that Hugo has been researching Le Dernier Souffle for 6 months and possesses old photographs of the vault. The timing of his article may have accelerated the heist.

evidence.title: Isabelle's Phone Records
evidence.description: It was discovered that Isabelle has been in contact with a black market dealer and 400,000 Euros have disappeared from her offshore account. The 3 million Euro commission she would receive if the painting is never found could save her.

evidence.title: Jean-Pierre's Security Schematic
evidence.description: It was revealed that Jean-Pierre discovered a 140,000 Euro gap in the museum budget and was fired when he asked Marguerite about it. The temporary access he gave Rémi before being fired was still active.

evidence.title: Marguerite's Gambling Debts
evidence.description: It was revealed that Marguerite has serious financial problems and carries heavy gambling debts. She may have paid a waiter 15,000 Euros to buy a false alibi. The insurance payout creates a double motive.

evidence.title: Nadia's Passport Records
evidence.description: Nadia's 3 trips to Moscow were confirmed, but on the 4th trip there are 3 missing days and a Zurich layover. A hidden copy of Le Dernier Souffle was found in her portfolio.

evidence.title: Rémi's Hidden Materials
evidence.description: A Le Dernier Souffle sketch and special pigments that could be used for forgery were found in Rémi's hidden cabinet. The workshop is accessible to Rémi, Nadia, Marguerite, and Isabelle.

scenes.text:
Paris. 3:00 AM. Fog hangs over the Seine and the marble facade of Musée des Lumières flickers in the blue-red glow of police lights.

3 days ago, the annual Nuit d'Or gala was held here. Champagne, tuxedos, paintings worth millions. Everything was perfect.

Until an expert visit changed everything. Prof. Laurent Dubois opened the vault to examine Caravaggio's lost masterpiece Le Dernier Souffle. And froze. The painting in the vault was a forgery.

The original, valued at 200 million Euros, is gone. Night security chief Lucien Moreau was arrested, but you, Detective, know it's not that simple.

The Paris Police Commissioner greets you: '8 suspects, 300 gala guests, 47 museum employees. This was an inside job, but by whom? Everyone has an alibi, everyone has a secret.'

choices.text: Review the gala guest list and badge data
Read the crime scene investigation report
Get a briefing from the security team

scenes.text:
You enter the museum's main gallery. A massive hall with 12-meter ceilings, paintings worth millions hanging on the walls. Police tape in the center.

The corridor to vault B-7 is on the right. The crime scene investigation team has finished their work.

Something on the floor catches your eye: a very small paint stain at the entrance to the corridor leading to the vault. Orange-brown, dried. This color wouldn't come from a normal visitor's shoes.

The empty frame on the wall awaits you. Le Dernier Souffle used to hang here; now a forged copy sits in the vault.

choices.text: Examine the forged painting under special light

scenes.addCharacterNote: Orange-brown paint stain in the corridor. Could be restoration material.

scenes.text:
The security control room is dark, the blue glow of monitors reflecting on your face. 16 screens show every corner of the museum. Or they're supposed to.

Technician Pierre greets you: 'Detective, I need to show you something.'

He pulls up the camera for the B-7 corridor. At 22:47 the image loops. At 22:58 it comes back to life. A full 11-minute gap.

'A loop,' Pierre says. 'Professional work. They overwrote the recording with earlier footage. You wouldn't catch it on normal playback; I had to run pixel analysis.'

Pierre points at the screen: 'And this was done from the internal network. Not from outside. One of the museum's own terminals was used.'

choices.text: Which terminals could have done this?

scenes.text:
You stand before vault B-7. Steel door, biometric panel, digital lock. Everything in place, no signs of forced entry.

You examine the vault logs:
- 22:49: Biometric scan SUCCESSFUL (Lucien Moreau)
- 22:49: Digital code CORRECT
- 22:49: Physical key USED
- 22:56: Vault LOCKED

7 minutes. Enough time to remove the painting and place the forgery.

But Lucien was in the security room at the time; camera recordings confirm this. How can his fingerprint be here?

There's a faint scratch on the wall beside the vault. As if something was dragged against it.

choices.text: Could the fingerprint have been cloned?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Vault was open for 7 min. Lucien's fingerprint is there but he was in the security room.

scenes.text:
You examine the forged painting under special light. Prof. Dubois is beside you:

'Incredible work,' he says with admiration. 'Same pigments, same canvas aging technique. Whoever did this has flawless command of 17th-century techniques.'

The ultraviolet light switches on: 'But here... the crack pattern from the original couldn't be perfectly replicated. There are very small differences. It took me 3 days to spot this forgery.'

Prof. Dubois turns to you: 'There aren't more than 10 people in the world who could produce a forgery of this quality. And sourcing these pigments requires special channels. Black market, private collectors, or art restoration networks.'

Estimated preparation time: at least 6 months.

choices.text: Ask Rémi who could have made the forgery

scenes.addCharacterNote: The forgery is of incredible quality. Made by an expert in 17th-century techniques. 6-month preparation time.

scenes.text:
Technician Pierre pulls up the internal network map.

'There are 4 terminals that can access the camera system: this security room, the museum director's office, the IT room, and the restoration workshop.'

He presses his finger against the screen: 'The IT room was locked on the night of the gala. Lucien and I were in the security room, but Lucien's card was cloned, so the security room terminal could have been accessed from outside too.'

He pauses: 'Actually, physical access to the terminal isn't even necessary. Jean-Pierre added a remote access module when he designed the system. The loop could be triggered remotely with the admin password. JP knew that password; maybe he shared it with others.'

'Whoever did the loop knows the system very well. A normal user couldn't perform this operation. Either someone who developed the software, someone who designed the system, or someone who got the password from one of them.'
scenes.addCharacterNote: Camera loop was done from internal network. One of 4 terminals was used: security room, director's office, IT room, restoration workshop.

scenes.text:
You examine the smart badge data from the gala night. Each guest was given a badge that tracked their movements inside the museum.

Notable movements during the critical 11 minutes (22:47-22:58):

Marguerite Delacroix: Left backstage at 22:40, returned at 22:58. 18 minutes unaccounted for.

Isabelle Chen: On Floor B from 22:20-22:55. 35 minutes. The same floor as the vault, fully covering the heist window.

Antoine Beaumont: On Floor B from 22:35-22:50. The same floor as the vault.

Nadia Sokolov: No badge signal from 22:30-22:55. 25 minutes, right in the middle of the heist.

Camille Laurent: Alone with Marguerite from 22:25-22:40. Then an encrypted phone call at 22:41.

Hugo Marchand: Tried to enter the security room at 22:45, was denied access.

Notable: The gaps for Marguerite, Isabelle, and Nadia nearly perfectly overlap with the heist window. All three were untraceable during the critical minutes.

choices.text: Question the unaccounted time gaps on gala night
Why was Antoine on Floor B?
What was Isabelle doing on Floor B for 35 min?

scenes.addCharacterNote: During the critical 11 minutes, 6 suspects were in suspicious locations. Everyone has an explanation, but none are fully convincing.

scenes.text:
Digital forensics expert Dr. Faure presents her report.

'We examined Lucien Moreau's security card. The card used at the restricted area entrance at 22:51 on gala night is an RFID copy of the original.'

A data table is projected on the screen: 'The original card was on Lucien at the time. The security room camera confirms this. So the card that opened the vault was a clone.'

Dr. Faure continues: 'The cloning was done at least 2 weeks prior. It requires physical proximity to the card and a specialized RFID reader. Anyone close to Lucien could have done it.'

This is important information. But it takes more to convince the prosecution.

choices.text: Who could have cloned the card?
Could the fingerprint have been cloned too?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Lucien's card was cloned.

scenes.text:
You investigate who knows the vault's digital code. According to official records, 3 people have this code:

1. Marguerite Delacroix, Museum Director
2. Lucien Moreau, Night Security Chief (arrested)
3. Pierre Mornet, Security Manager (on vacation in Marseille on gala night, confirmed)

BUT dig deeper and it gets complicated:

Marguerite wrote the code in a notebook inside her office safe. People who entered the office in the last 6 months: Isabelle (insurance audit), Camille (exhibition planning meeting), Hugo (interview), Antoine (painting offer discussion). Any of them could have seen the code.

Also, Jean-Pierre is the person who designed the system. His knowledge of temporary codes and bypass methods may still be valid.

So it's not 'just 3 people'--potentially 7-8 people could access this code.

choices.text: Question those who entered the office in the last 6 months

scenes.addCharacterNote: Vault code officially known by 3 people but written in a notebook. Anyone who entered the office could have seen it.

scenes.text:
Marguerite Delacroix receives you in her luxurious office. Monet and Degas reproductions on the walls, a museum model on her desk. Her silver hair is immaculately styled, her black dress elegant.

'Detective, this museum is my life. I've been here for 25 years. This heist is devastating to me as well.'

She takes a sip of coffee, her hand steady:

'On gala night I was on stage. I gave speeches, shook hands with the Minister of Culture. I have hundreds of witnesses.'

Marguerite is calm and composed. She projects a professional demeanor. But is this much composure normal?

choices.text: Where were you for those 18 minutes?
What do you think about Lucien?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Marguerite is calm and composed. Says she was on stage at the gala but 18 min unaccounted for.

scenes.text:
You enter the restoration workshop. The smell of paint, old canvases, brushes. The lighting is dim but the work desk is well-lit.

On the desk is a half-finished Renoir cleaning. This is what Rémi was working on during the gala.

When the workshop is searched: breaking open a cabinet in the back room reveals two types of materials.

On one side: 3 canvases aged using 17th-century techniques, Caravaggio-era pigment mixtures, special chemical compounds for creating crack patterns under ultraviolet light. And one unfinished sketch: a compositional study of Le Dernier Souffle.

On the other side: a 120-page academic paper draft, 'Caravaggio Pigment Analysis'. Prepared for submission to Sorbonne University. He has a previously published paper in the JCS journal (Vasseur, R., 2024).

The cabinet key is on Rémi's personal keychain. But a striking detail: according to workshop entry logs, 3 other people regularly entered this room in the last 6 months besides Rémi--Nadia (internship work), Isabelle (insurance audit), and Marguerite (management inspection). A spare copy of the cabinet key is in the workshop safe, accessible to everyone.

Are the materials for forgery or academic research? And who used them?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Le Dernier Souffle sketch found in hidden cabinet. But Nadia's portfolio also has an unfinished copy of the same painting! Both worked on the same painting. Workshop accessible to Rémi, Nadia, Marguerite, and Isabelle.

scenes.text:
Rémi Vasseur meets you in the workshop. Thin build, paint-stained hands, a quiet man. He sets down his brush and sits in a chair.

'Detective... I'm just a restorer. I've been working at this museum for 15 years.'

He speaks calmly, measured:

'On gala night I was here cleaning a Renoir. I was alone. I don't get involved with galas; I stay in the background.'

He maintains a professional distance.

'I know Lucien, we're from the same town. But people change. I hadn't been seeing much of him lately; he kept to himself.'

choices.text: What are the materials in the hidden cabinet?
Did you really go to Auvergne 6 months ago?
Describe the work environment at the museum

scenes.addCharacterNote: Rémi is tense but Prof. Marchetti confirms his academic work. From the same town as Lucien but said 'people change'--is he distancing himself or feeling guilt?

scenes.text:
You find Isabelle Chen in a hotel lobby. Stylish suit, sharp gaze, a thick file in her hand.

'Detective, I want to help you. I'm an insurance inspector; investigating incidents like this is my job.'

She pulls a page from her file:

'The museum's insurance policy is 200 million Euros. If the painting isn't found, the insurance company will pay out. Think about who that money goes to.'

She adjusts her glasses: 'On gala night, I was conducting a routine audit on the floor where the vault is. I do it every year on behalf of the insurance company. But I saw strange things that night.'

She leans forward: 'Antoine Beaumont was wandering around the corridor where the vault is. It was around 22:35.'

choices.text: What were you doing on that floor?
Can I see your phone records?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Isabelle says she saw Antoine on Floor B. But she was there too.

scenes.text:
Antoine Beaumont awaits you at a cafe on the banks of the Seine. 62 years old, elegant suit, silver cane. An espresso and an art magazine on his table.

'Detective, I've been collecting art for 40 years. Le Dernier Souffle was my white whale. I've been trying to buy it for 20 years; rejected every time.'

He twirls his cane:

'Gala night? I was drinking champagne at the bar. The waiters will remember me; I leave good tips.'

But the smart badge data places him on Floor B at 22:35.

'Ah, yes. I was looking for the restroom. This museum's layout is like a labyrinth.'

There are no restrooms on Floor B. The museum map confirms this.

choices.text: There are no restrooms on Floor B, you know that
About your obsession with the painting
About the hidden room in your home

scenes.addCharacterNote: Antoine was seen on Floor B, says 'I was looking for the restroom' but there are no restrooms there.

scenes.text:
Nadia Sokolov is sitting in the museum cafeteria. 29 years old, light brown hair, anxious eyes. She keeps turning her bracelet.

'I was on duty at the gala, helping guests. I went to the restroom at one point, that's all.'

But the badge data shows a 25-minute gap. The signal was completely lost.

'The badge? I probably took it off; it was uncomfortable. Then I put it back on.'

What you know about Nadia: She trained in reproduction at the Paris School of Fine Arts. Her professor, Prof. Girard: 'Nadia is my most talented reproduction artist. Her portfolio contains 6 different Old Master copies.' She has daily access to the workshop as an intern.

5 days before the heist, she was caught photographing restricted areas. 'I was shooting for my portfolio,' she says, but the photos show security camera angles.

Her father is Dmitri Sokolov. Former KGB agent. Connected to an Eastern European art theft ring. Nadia's laptop contains her father's old heist files. 'I'm researching my father's past,' she says, but those files also serve as a heist methodology manual.

choices.text: How did your relationship with Lucien end?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Nadia unaccounted for 25 min, removed her badge. Has reproduction training, 6 Old Master copies in portfolio. Father is ex-KGB. But concern for father is real--Alzheimer's treatment confirmed.

scenes.text:
You find Jean-Pierre Dumas at a cafe near an old antique shop. Tired, bearded, sleeves rolled up. A beer and a cigarette on his table.

'I was fired 6 months ago. For 15 years I designed, updated, and maintained that museum's security system. Then the new director came, Marguerite, and she showed me the door.'

He squeezes his beer glass:

'Incompetence, they said. Incompetence! I built that system from scratch. Every camera, every sensor, every alarm currently running passed through my hands.'

He looks at you: 'I didn't even go near the museum that night. I sat at the cafe across the street and drank beer. But let me tell you this: whoever did that loop knows my system very well. Very well.'

choices.text: What are the security schematics in your home?
Who could have done this loop?
Why did you leave the museum?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Jean-Pierre is very angry at Marguerite. He designed the system. Said 'whoever did the loop knows my system.'

scenes.text:
Camille Laurent's auction house is on the Champs-Elysees. Luxury, marble, paintings worth millions on the walls. Camille receives you in her office. Black dress, pearl necklace, a cold smile.

'Detective, I was networking at the gala. I spoke with dozens of people. I didn't even go near the vault.'

She offers tea:

'This heist has shaken the auction world. If a Caravaggio disappears, it disrupts the market.'

She looks at you: 'Do you know Isabelle Chen? The insurance inspector. She was on the vault floor that night. Interesting, isn't it?'

But you know that Camille spoke privately with Marguerite for 15 minutes at the gala.

choices.text: Who did you speak with at the gala and about what?
Are you business partners with Antoine Beaumont?
Explain the Zurich crate in your warehouse

scenes.addCharacterNote: Camille is casting suspicion on Isabelle. Spoke privately with Marguerite for 15 min.

scenes.text:
Hugo Marchand's studio is messy. Photos on the walls, newspaper clippings, notes connected with red string. A classic investigator's den.

'I'd been researching Le Dernier Souffle for 6 months. The painting's provenance is dubious; its lost period in the 1940s was never explained.'

He lights a cigarette:

'2 weeks ago I published my article: Is This Painting Real? It sparked a major controversy. Marguerite was furious, said she'd accuse me in court.'

He looks at you: 'I was the first to break the heist story. How did I find out? Sources, Detective. Journalists have their sources.'

But Hugo's computer contains photos of the vault. Taken BEFORE the gala.

choices.text: What are these photos of the vault?
Is the timing of the article a coincidence?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Hugo has been researching the painting for 6 months. Article timing is very suspicious. Has old photos of the vault.

scenes.text:
You examine the director's office. Luxurious, orderly, everything in its place. Museum documents on the desk, a calendar, a pen set.

In the desk drawer: Restoration reports. The last 2 years' reports are here. Most are standard technical jargon.

But the 'Technical Notes' section in some reports contains strange phrases:

'Pigment 7 ready.'
'Frame B compatible.'
'Timing November suitable.'

They look like normal technical terms. But when arranged chronologically, a pattern emerges. These phrases don't match routine restoration work.

The reports pass through different departments: the writer, the approver, the archivist. Everyone in this chain could be in the know. The reports date back 2 years.

choices.text: Question the person who approved the reports
Investigate the person who wrote the reports
Talk to Nadia, she has access to reports as an intern

scenes.addCharacterNote: Strange technical notes in restoration reports: 'Pigment 7 ready', 'Frame B compatible'. Normal or coded communication?

scenes.text:
Lucien Moreau is in the prison visiting room. 34 years old but looking 50 now. Dark circles under his eyes, hair disheveled.

'I didn't do it, Detective. I swear I didn't.'

His hands tremble on the table:

'That night I was in the security room. I was watching the cameras. I didn't see any anomaly because the loop was undetectable--professional work.'

His eyes well up: 'The money in my account is from my mother's house. We'd been waiting for the sale for 3 months. The timing is terrible, but that's the truth.'

He leans toward you: 'Listen to me. Someone inside this museum did this. Not from outside. Someone who knows the vault code, knows the system, has access to the paintings. I'm just the security chief; I don't even have access to the paintings.'

choices.text: Your card was cloned, did you know?
Who do you suspect among the 8 suspects?
How did your relationship with Nadia end?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Lucien appears innocent. House sale is documented. Says 'someone inside did it.'

scenes.text:
Who could have cloned Lucien's card? Physical contact with the card is required.

People close to Lucien:
- Nadia: could have accessed his card during their romantic relationship
- Marguerite: as his boss, could get close to him anytime
- Rémi: childhood friend, together in social settings
- Jean-Pierre: may have handled the card while training Lucien on the system

Specialized equipment is needed for RFID cloning. Who could have obtained that equipment?

choices.text: Question those who know the vault code
Could the fingerprint have been cloned too?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Card cloning: Nadia, Marguerite, Rémi, or Jean-Pierre could have done it.

scenes.text:
Dr. Faure explains about the fingerprint:

'Cloning a biometric fingerprint is much harder than RFID. But not impossible. There's the silicone mold method. Lifting Lucien's fingerprint from a surface and creating a silicone mold...'

She pauses: 'You'd need a flat surface Lucien touched. Like a glass, a file folder cover. And patience.'

This information is important: card cloning + fingerprint cloning = a completely forged identity. Whoever did this is close to Lucien and has technical knowledge.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Fingerprint may have been cloned too. Using silicone mold method. Someone close to Lucien.

scenes.text:
Marguerite's face freezes for a moment. She adjusts her silver hair.

'18 minutes? Ah, yes... I had a migraine. I rested backstage for a bit. There's a sofa there; I sometimes take a break.'

She averts her eyes: 'It's nothing major, gala stress. 300 guests, the Minister of Culture, the press...'

But 18 minutes is more than enough time for a painting swap. And Marguerite is one of only 3 people who know the vault code.

'Do you consider me a suspect?' Her voice is ice cold. 'Would I risk my 25-year career?'

choices.text: What do you think about the employees?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Marguerite explained the 18-min gap as 'migraine.' Not convincing.

scenes.text:
Marguerite answers with a thoughtful expression.

'The vault code... Only I, Lucien, and Pierre know it. Pierre was on vacation, confirmed.'

She pauses for a moment: 'But the code was changed 6 months ago. Jean-Pierre knew the old code, before he was fired. He shouldn't know the new one, but... Jean-Pierre is a clever man.'

She raises a finger: 'One more thing. The codes are stored encrypted in the digital system. But I have a bad habit of writing them down in a notebook in my office safe. Anyone near my desk could see it.'

This is an important confession. Marguerite may have left the code exposed.

choices.text: Open the safe in your office

scenes.addCharacterNote: Marguerite wrote the vault code in a notebook in her office! Anyone nearby could have seen it.

scenes.text:
Marguerite's face tenses.

'Lucien... I hired him, 5 years ago. He was a hard worker.'

She walks to the window, turning her back: 'The evidence is clear. Fingerprint, card, money in his account. What do you want me to say?'

A moment of silence. Then a whisper: 'Everyone in this museum has secrets, Detective. Lucien, me, the others. Instead of asking who used whom, ask who stands to gain the most.'

Marguerite doesn't directly accuse anyone, but what does she mean by 'everyone has secrets'?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Marguerite didn't directly accuse anyone but said 'everyone has secrets' and 'ask who gains the most.' What is she hiding?

scenes.text:
You examine the restoration workshop's material invoices.

Normal supplies: brushes, solvents, protective varnishes. All from the museum budget.

But 3 invoices stand out:
- 18 months ago: 'Special pigment mixture, 17th-century formulation' - 4,200 Euros
- 12 months ago: 'Aged canvas, 3 units' - 2,800 Euros
- 6 months ago: 'UV-reactive chemical compound set' - 3,100 Euros

Total: 10,100 Euros. All invoiced as 'restoration materials.' Approved by: Marguerite Delacroix.

Are these standard restoration supplies, or meant for another purpose?

choices.text: Ask Marguerite about the coded reports

scenes.addCharacterNote: Forgery materials were invoiced from the museum budget. Approval process needs investigation.

scenes.text:
You examine the back wall of the workshop. Old wooden panels. One has a slightly open edge.

You push it. The panel slides and a dark passage appears. The service corridor. Narrow, damp, unmaintained, but there are fresh footprints on the floor.

You follow the corridor. After 40 meters you reach another panel. You open it: you emerge in the area where vault B-7 is located.

This corridor doesn't appear on the museum's old floor plans. But someone knew about it and used it.

The footprints on the floor have traces of paint residue: orange-brown tones. The forensic team takes samples. The lab report will take time, but this color could come from multiple sources in the museum: restoration materials, exhibition paints, even building maintenance products. Who knows about the corridor is also unclear; anyone who's worked at the museum for 15+ years could be aware of this passage.

choices.text: Question Rémi about the pigment analysis

scenes.addCharacterNote: Hidden service corridor: direct passage from workshop to vault. Paint residue found, lab report pending, multiple possible sources. People who knew about corridor: Rémi, JP, Marguerite, Antoine (old tour), Nadia (museum plans).

scenes.text:
Rémi meets you in his workshop. He sets down his brush, wipes his hands.

'I'm running a joint academic project with the Sorbonne. Caravaggio pigment analysis. I have a published paper in the Journal of Conservation Science, Vasseur 2024.'

He points to papers on his desk: 'A 120-page paper draft. Every restorer does reproduction studies; compositional analysis is essential to understanding the original. Every art school teaches this.'

He thinks: 'But I didn't use it that night. I was in the workshop, cleaning a Renoir. Philippe saw me too.'

'Multiple people know about that corridor. JP discovered it while designing the system, Antoine used it during a tour 20 years ago, Nadia can access the museum plans.'

Remi is calm but distant. 'Multiple people know about this corridor. It's unfair to single me out.' he says finally.

As he gets up, you glance at the materials on the desk: the workshop has various period pigments - Renoir, Caravaggio, Vermeer. Standard for a restoration workshop?

choices.text: Note the pigment stain and investigate the corridor

scenes.addCharacterNote: Remi admitted he knows about the corridor but says he didn't use it. The pigments in the workshop are noteworthy.

scenes.text:
You ask about the large bag Antoine brought up to his hotel room.

'That? It's the carrying case for two Caravaggio studies I showed Camille before the gala. The museum management told me it wasn't for sale but I was going to leave an offer anyway. Collector's passion, you understand.'

You examine the bag. An art carrying case with adjustable inner compartments. Currently arranged for small frames, but if you remove the dividers, a large painting could fit too. Antoine becomes uncomfortable when he notices you've realized this.

'What are you trying to imply? That bag has always been arranged that way!'
scenes.addCharacterNote: Antoine's bag has removable compartments, a large painting could fit. Antoine became uneasy when questioned about this.

scenes.text:
You ask Nadia to describe the person who came out of the workshop in more detail.

'Tall, thin build. Walking hurriedly but not running. Carrying something, like a large flat case. The face... I couldn't quite see, the corridor was dark. But the way they moved, their walk seemed familiar. Could be someone who works at the museum. Hair... was dark, I think.'

This description could match multiple people working at the museum: Remi, Jean-Pierre, even Hugo.

choices.text: Investigate the person who left the workshop

scenes.addCharacterNote: Nadia saw someone exit the workshop: tall, thin, dark-haired. Matches multiple suspects.

scenes.text:
You request information from Russian authorities to verify Nadia's Moscow trips.

A few hours later, the report arrives: Nadia Sokolov visited the Moscow Neurology Center 4 times in the last 6 months. Hospital records match for 3 visits, Prof. Andrei Volkov, Alzheimer's specialist. Patient: Dmitri Sokolov, 68 years old, Nadia's father.

However, the 4th visit is noteworthy: Nadia stayed for 5 days but the hospital record only covers 2 days. Where was she for the remaining 3 days? No hotel record, flight ticket to Moscow but the return wasn't to Paris, she flew to Zurich first, then to Paris.

Nadia's father is truly ill. But is every Moscow trip just a visit to her father?

choices.text: Who could have cloned Lucien's card?

scenes.addCharacterNote: 3 of Nadia's Moscow trips were verified but the 4th trip has 3 missing days and a Zurich transfer. Is everything really for her father?

scenes.text:
You ask Nadia how long she kept Lucien's security card.

'Only one night! Lucien forgot his card, I found it at the door and gave it back the next morning. Not even 12 hours.'

'But someone could have cloned the card during those 12 hours.'

Nadia's eyes fill with tears. 'I left the card in my room and went to sleep. I gave it to Lucien in the morning. I hadn't even thought it could have been cloned.'

Something catches your attention: you remember seeing an RFID reader device in Nadia's room. Interns use it for inventory scanning, but the same device could be used to copy a card. Does Nadia know this?

choices.text: Who could find RFID cloning equipment?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Nadia kept the card for one night. There's an RFID reader in her room, for inventory but cloning is also possible. Does she have the technical knowledge?

scenes.text:
You ask Jean-Pierre about the suspicious budget items.

'Ah, yes I saw them. Marguerite wrote 180,000 Euros in the budget for 'special restoration materials'. But I know the prices of those materials, they're worth 40,000 Euros at most. Where did the remaining 140,000 go? I got fired when I asked about it.'

'Do you have documentation?'

'I have copies at home. But I didn't go to the police because who would believe a fired employee over Paris's most respected museum director?'

Jean-Pierre leans forward: 'Let me give you some advice. Go to Marguerite, ask her about the gambling problem to her face. That woman is in deep trouble, she's lost millions at Monaco casinos. The 180,000 Euro budget gap is just the beginning.'

choices.text: Talk to Marguerite about the gambling debts
Who were these bypass codes shared with?

scenes.addCharacterNote: JP found a 140,000 Euro budget gap. Marguerite fired him when he asked about it.

scenes.text:
You ask Jean-Pierre who he shared the security system bypass codes with.

'The bypass codes were only with me and museum director Marguerite. We only gave Lucien user-level access, not bypass. But someone who knows the system codes could also get in through the terminal in the restoration workshop. That terminal is connected to the internal network.'

'Yes. I once gave Remi temporary access for a system update. But I may not have revoked the temporary access... My dismissal was very sudden, I couldn't close out all my processes.'
scenes.addCharacterNote: JP gave Remi temporary access and didn't revoke it before being fired. Multiple people including Remi could have used this access.

scenes.text:
You ask Jean-Pierre how he knows about Marguerite's gambling debts.

'A casino manager in Monaco is an old friend of mine. One day he told me: 'Your old boss Delacroix spent a fortune here, went bust.' So I did some digging. She has over 3 million Euros in debt. She borrowed from loan sharks, with interest she needs to pay an impossible amount.'

'Why didn't you report this to the police?'

'Who would I tell? Would the Paris police believe that Marguerite Delacroix is suspicious? I'm a fired employee, it's my word against hers.'
scenes.addCharacterNote: JP has a casino manager friend in Monaco. Marguerite's debts confirmed.

scenes.text:
You ask Hugo what he knows about Marguerite.

'Marguerite Delacroix? There are very interesting things about that woman. First of all, her finances are disastrous. Casino debts, loan sharks... How do I know? I'm an investigative journalist, sources talk before I even ask.'

'Do you think she's connected to the heist?'

'Let me put it this way: a painting worth 200 million Euros is stolen, and the museum director has 3 million Euros in debt... You do the math. But I can't write that, I have no proof. Just suspicion.'

choices.text: Give information about your anonymous source
Call in museum management for questioning

scenes.addCharacterNote: Hugo knows about Marguerite's gambling debts. He has sources as an investigative journalist.

scenes.text:
You ask Hugo who made the anonymous call.

'I can't reveal my sources, journalistic ethics. But I can tell you this: the caller knew for certain the painting was fake. Not just a guess, they gave technical details. Specific information like the crack pattern not matching under ultraviolet light.'

'Who could know this?'

'Someone who examined the painting up close. A restorer, an expert, or... the person who made the fake. Maybe they felt guilty? Maybe the fake needed to be discovered for the insurance money?'

This is a very important clue. Anyone who closely examined the painting could have this information: Remi during restoration, Nadia during her internship, Isabelle during insurance inspection, even Marguerite as museum director. Who was the caller?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Hugo's anonymous source gave technical details. The caller is someone from inside the museum who knew the painting was fake.

scenes.text:
You ask Lucien what he knows about Marguerite.

'Marguerite was my boss, we spoke every day. In recent months she was very tense. She was constantly fighting with someone on the phone, not in French but another language. Once when I walked into her office, she hung up very quickly and gave me a stern look.'

'Did you notice anything else strange?'

'There was tension in management. Marguerite was frequently meeting with Isabelle behind closed doors, they said it was for insurance audits but the tone was never routine. After Jean-Pierre was fired, extra meetings with several people began. I couldn't keep track of who was talking to whom about what anymore. Everyone at the museum seemed to suspect each other.'
scenes.addCharacterNote: Lucien: Marguerite was tense, was frequently meeting with Isabelle. After JP was fired, everyone at the museum suspected each other.

scenes.text:
You request a confidential confirmation from the Interpol headquarters in Lyon to verify Isabelle's Interpol connection.

A few hours later, an encrypted message arrives: 'Agent Chen is registered under Operation Clair de Lune. However, the operation status has not been updated for 3 months. Viktor Petrov's informant file has been suspended, he has not submitted his last report.'

Isabelle may be working with Interpol, but whether the operation is active is unclear. Petrov's file is suspended. Is Isabelle acting on her own? Or is she using the Interpol mission for personal gain?

choices.text: Talk to Isabelle about Antoine's suspicious offers

scenes.addCharacterNote: Isabelle is registered at Interpol but the operation hasn't been updated for 3 months. Petrov's file is suspended. Is she acting on her own?

scenes.text:
[2 MONTHS AGO - MARGUERITE'S OFFICE]

Marguerite sits at her desk, a pile of documents before her. Some are financial reports, some are sponsorship files. She looks stressed but the reason is unclear.

Her phone rings. A short conversation, only Marguerite's side is audible:

'Yes... Everything must be ready for the November gala.'
'We must not exceed the budget. The board will ask questions.'
'Okay, we'll talk.'

She hangs up. A routine work call, or something else? One of the documents on the desk ended up under a file she quickly closed.
scenes.addCharacterNote: FLASHBACK: Marguerite was making a stressful phone call 2 months ago. Gala preparation or something else?

scenes.text:
[6 MONTHS AGO - RESTORATION WORKSHOP, NIGHT]

Someone is working alone in a dark workshop. Before them are reference photos and a canvas. What they're doing is unclear, restoration work, a copy, or their own project?

There are some papers on the desk but the details can't be read in the dark. November is marked on a calendar, with some names and question marks next to it.

The person pauses for a moment, looks at their canvas, then continues.

Their face is in darkness. It could be anyone with access to the workshop.
scenes.addCharacterNote: FLASHBACK: Someone was working in the workshop at night 6 months ago. What they were doing and who they were is unclear.

scenes.text:
[GALA NIGHT - 22:40]

The gala hall is overflowing with energy. 300 guests raise their champagne glasses.

At the same time, on the lower floor:
22:42 - A shadow descends through the service corridor.
22:45 - The camera loop is activated from a terminal on the internal network.
22:47 - Cameras go into a loop. 11 minutes.
22:49 - The restricted area is entered with a cloned card.
22:51 - The original painting is removed, the fake is placed.
22:56 - The vault is locked again.
22:58 - The camera loop ends.

Upstairs the gala continues. Nobody notices a thing.
scenes.addCharacterNote: FLASHBACK: Gala night chronology. The thief used the service corridor and internal network terminal. Who?

scenes.text:
One of the waiters who worked at the gala, Marc Duval, wants to give a statement.

'Detective, I saw something important. Marguerite Delacroix was in the bar area between 22:40 and 22:50. She ordered champagne. I personally served her. She had no migraine or anything, she was sitting perfectly fine on a bar stool.'

This statement could completely close Marguerite's 18-minute gap. If the waiter is telling the truth, Marguerite was sitting at the bar during those critical minutes and it would have been impossible for her to go to the vault.

But can the waiter's statement be verified? Was the bar camera also put on a loop? And is the waiter's background clean?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Waiter Marc Duval claims he saw Marguerite at the bar between 22:40-22:50. Must be verified!

scenes.text:
Waiter Marc Duval's background is being investigated.

The results are mixed: Marc Duval went bankrupt 2 years ago due to casino debt. 15,000 Euros in cash was deposited into Duval's bank account 3 days ago. Source unknown.

Interesting detail: Duval also served Antoine Beaumont that evening and claims Antoine was 'sitting at the bar all night'. So Duval is providing an alibi for both Marguerite and Antoine. Were they both at the bar at the same time, or is Duval lying for one, or both, of them?

Bar camera examination: The bar area was also affected during the camera loop. The bar camera was in a loop between 22:40-22:50. Cannot be verified.

Who deposited the 15,000 Euros? Marguerite, Antoine, or a third party?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Waiter Duval received 15,000 Euros and is providing an alibi for both Marguerite and Antoine. Whose behalf is he lying on? Or both?

scenes.text:
You investigate the business relationship between Antoine and Camille in depth.

Auction house records: In the last 5 years, 12 of Antoine's major purchases went through Camille's auction house. Total value: 28 million Euros. But 3 purchases are suspicious: the paintings were sent not to Antoine's home, but to Camille's warehouse in Marseille. Why?

Encrypted message analysis: Messages from Camille's encrypted phone to Antoine were recovered:
- 'Painting ready. Transfer in November.'
- 'Buyer confirmed. Via Zurich.'
- 'We meet after the gala. The usual place.'

And a security camera image: 2 nights before the gala, at 23:00, Antoine and Camille meet secretly at the back door of Camille's auction house. Antoine arrives carrying a large bag.

Could this duo also be behind the heist? Or are they running a different scheme?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Secret paintings, encrypted messages, and a night meeting between Antoine and Camille! A parallel conspiracy?

scenes.text:
The Marseille warehouse investigation is complete.

The 3 suspicious paintings inside the warehouse were examined. Two are temporary storage for legitimate purchases, Camille's auction house sends large pieces to Marseille for restoration. Documents are clean.

However, the third painting is problematic: No registration number, missing provenance documentation, and its dimensions are very close to Le Dernier Souffle. Camille explains this as 'a piece left by a former client' but no client name is given.

The 'buyer' in the encrypted messages is a private collection in Zurich, appears legal but the same collection has been investigated for stolen art trade before.

The midnight meeting: Antoine was supposedly discussing the purchase strategy for a Monet painting with Camille. But why at midnight, why with encrypted messages?

choices.text: Note the warehouse evidence and continue

scenes.addCharacterNote: 2 paintings in the Antoine-Camille warehouse are clean but the 3rd has no record and suspicious dimensions. The Zurich buyer was previously investigated. Night meetings still unexplained.

scenes.text:
Museum security guard Philippe Bernard gives his statement.

'Remi Vasseur was in the restoration workshop all evening on the night of the gala. I pass through the corridor every 30 minutes. 21:00, 21:30, 22:00, 22:30, 23:00 - every time the workshop light was on and Remi was working at his desk. I saw him 5 times.'

This statement completely clears Remi! If Philippe saw Remi every 30 minutes, it means Remi never left the workshop.

But the critical question: Philippe didn't pass through the corridor between 22:30 and 23:00. This 30-minute gap encompasses precisely the 22:47-22:58 window when the heist occurred.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Security guard Philippe claims he saw Remi in the workshop all night.

scenes.text:
Philippe starts sweating.

'Between 22:30 and 23:00? I... was in the corridor. As usual.'

You insist. The security cameras (those not put on a loop) are being examined.

'Okay, okay!' Philippe confesses. 'I went to the restroom during that time. My stomach was upset, I'd eaten something at the gala. About 11 minutes... maybe it was 15 minutes. But when I came back, Remi was still in the workshop!'

11 minutes. Exactly the duration of the camera loop. During this gap, anyone moving through the museum could reach the vault unnoticed: Remi from the workshop via the service corridor, Nadia was also missing during those hours (25 min), Marguerite from her office (18 min missing), even JP with the old admin password. Philippe's absence creates an opportunity for multiple suspects.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Philippe was in the restroom for 11 minutes! During this gap, multiple suspects could have reached the vault: Remi, Nadia, Marguerite, JP. Everyone's alibi weakened.

scenes.text:
You examine the gala guest list and smart badge data. 300 guests, 47 museum staff, 12 press members.

Antoine Beaumont (Collector): On Floor B between 22:35-22:50. The floor where the vault is. Says he was looking for a restroom but there's no restroom on Floor B.
Nadia Sokolov (Intern): Badge signal completely lost between 22:30-22:55. Either left or removed and left it.
Camille Laurent (Auctioneer): One-on-one meeting with Marguerite between 22:25-22:40. Then alone between 22:45-23:00, making 3 international calls from an encrypted phone.
Isabelle Chen (Insurance Inspector): On Floor B between 22:20-22:55 for 'routine inspection'. 35 minutes on the floor where the vault is.

scenes.text:
The crime scene investigation report is detailed:

1. VAULT: No signs of forced entry. Opened with biometric + digital code + physical key. Opening record belongs to Lucien Moreau but Lucien was in the security room.

2. FAKE PAINTING: Incredible quality. 17th-century techniques used. Preparation time at least 6 months. The number of people in the world who could work at this quality doesn't exceed 10 - professional restorer, art forger, or reproduction specialist.

3. CAMERA LOOP: B-7 corridor cameras were looped between 22:47-22:58. Operation was performed from the internal network. 4 terminals can access: security room, director's office, IT room, restoration workshop.

4. PAINT STAIN IN CORRIDOR: Orange-brown, dried. Source unknown - could be restoration material, art paint, or industrial paint. Lab analysis pending.

5. FINGERPRINT: Only Lucien's print on the vault panel. But RFID analysis shows the card is a CLONE.

choices.text: Examine the fake painting with an expert
Talk to the insurance inspector - there are interesting details in the policy

scenes.addCharacterNote: Crime scene report: cloned card, professional forgery, camera loop from internal network. A planned operation.

scenes.text:
Deputy security chief Pierre Mornet connects via video conference (on vacation, in Marseille):

'3 people know the vault code: me, Lucien, and Marguerite. BUT during a security update 6 months ago, temporary codes were created. Jean-Pierre also had access to these temporary codes - before he was fired. The codes were changed but since Jean-Pierre designed the original system, he knows bypass methods.'

Technician Pierre (security room, on-site): 'The camera loop was performed from the internal network. 4 terminals can access it. But one more thing: the person who made the loop knew the admin password. Only 2 people know this password: me and Jean-Pierre. I was here that night, my records are clean.'

Security guard Philippe Bernard enters: 'I was on corridor duty that night. I need to tell you something but... I need to talk to my lawyer first.'

Philippe looks uneasy. What is he hiding?

choices.text: Investigate who knew the admin password

You examine Floor B. The corridor where the vault is located is on the right. 3 people were here during the critical hours:

Antoine Beaumont: Badge data 22:35-22:50, 15 minutes on Floor B. Says 'I was looking for a restroom' but there's no restroom on Floor B. Has been trying to buy this painting for 20 years, 7 offers rejected. Handwritten on his last offer note: 'This painting will be mine, I'll find a way.'

choices.text: Talk to Antoine - how far did the obsession go?
Talk to Isabelle - who is Petrov?
Talk to Nadia - about her father

scenes.text:
The digital forensics team examines the phone traffic from the gala night.

Notable calls:

  22:30 Zurich (+41) - 4 minutes
  23:15 Dubai (+971) - 7 minutes
  23:45 Hong Kong (+852) - 3 minutes

scenes.text:
You trace the private line. The number is registered to a prepaid SIM card, place of purchase: Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport, 2 weeks ago.

The SIM card purchase camera is being examined: the footage shows a hooded figure, face not visible. Height estimate: between 165-175cm. Suspects matching this height range: Isabelle (168cm), Nadia (170cm), Camille (172cm), Hugo (174cm).

But Marguerite is also 169cm. And Remi is 178cm - he could appear shorter if leaning forward in a hood.

scenes.addCharacterNote: Prepaid SIM: hooded buyer, height 165-175cm. Fits 5+ suspects. Contacted Marguerite twice.

scenes.text:
The street cameras around the museum are being examined.

At 01:00 on the night of the gala, a black minibus parks 200 meters from the museum's back door. License plate reading: HA-2847-KL.

License plate search: The vehicle is registered to Camille Laurent's auction house!

But the records are mixed up: The vehicle was sold 2 months ago. Buyer: 'Marcel Dupont' - fake identity, address is an empty lot. Camille: 'I sold that vehicle, I have the documents, it went through the notary.'

The minibus remained parked between 01:00-03:00 on the night of the gala. Then it left. What was transported inside?

Even more interesting: the same minibus was spotted in Isabelle's hotel parking lot 1 week earlier. Isabelle: 'I never saw that vehicle.'
scenes.addCharacterNote: Black minibus: Camille's old vehicle, sold with a fake identity. Also spotted at Isabelle's hotel. Who is using it?

scenes.text:
While investigating the Marcel Dupont fake identity, a match comes up in the Interpol database: the same fake identity pattern was used by an Eastern European art theft ring in the 1990s.

Ring leader: Dmitri Sokolov. Nadia's father.

Dmitri's file is opened:

- 1995: Vienna Albertina, Durer stolen - inside accomplice + fake painting
- 1998: Warsaw National Museum, Rembrandt stolen - same method

All used the SAME METHOD: inside accomplice + fake painting swap + camera manipulation.

Identical to the Musee des Lumieres heist.

M.D. = Marguerite Delacroix? Marcel Dupont? Someone else?

scenes.text:
The M.D. investigation hits a dead end but something else emerges.

Isabelle Chen's insurance company has paid out 3 major art theft claims in the last 5 years:
- 2021: London, Turner painting - 45M Euros - FAKE PAINTING SWAP
- 2023: Milan, Botticelli study - 28M Euros - INSIDE ACCOMPLICE
- 2025: Paris, Caravaggio - 200M Euros - ongoing

Isabelle was the auditor in all three cases. All three used the SAME METHOD.

The London case is still unsolved. The Milan case: the inside accomplice was caught but the 'real mastermind' was never found.

Isabelle was at the scene for 'routine inspection' in all three cases. Isabelle's company earns commission as it pays claims: 1.5% = total 4.1M Euros.

Is Isabelle the mastermind? Or is her Interpol story real and is she there to investigate these cases?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Isabelle was the auditor in 3 major art thefts! All the same method. Commission total 4.1M Euros. Coincidence?

scenes.text:
The London case file is opened. The Turner painting heist, 2021. Fake painting swap + camera manipulation.

Shocking detail: The firm that designed the London museum's security system: SecureArt Paris. Founder and chief engineer: Jean-Pierre Dumas.

Jean-Pierre also designed the London museum's security system! And that museum was also robbed!

Even more interesting: The digital metadata of the 'November Plan' file found in Jean-Pierre's home is being examined. File creation date: 8 months ago. Last modified: 3 days before the gala.

And a photograph: 1 week before the gala, at a cafe in Montmartre, Jean-Pierre and Isabelle sitting side by side. Camera footage.

Jean-Pierre: 'Isabelle asked me for security consultation. For her insurance firm. Normal.'
Isabelle: 'I got technical information from JP. For my Interpol report.'

Their explanations match. BUT both had been acting as if they didn't know each other. Why are they now telling the same story?

choices.text: Question the security schematics in Jean-Pierre's home

scenes.addCharacterNote: JP also designed the London museum's security and it was also robbed! JP + Isabelle met secretly. Strongest alternative theory.

scenes.text:
Philippe Bernard finally confesses.

'Okay, I'll tell you everything. 2 days before the gala, I found an envelope inside my locker. Inside was 500 Euros and a note: Leave the corridor empty between 22:30-23:00. Don't ask.'

'Who left the envelope?'

'I don't know! Anyone can open the locker, it's an old model.'

The serial numbers of the 500 Euro banknotes are being checked... Result: These banknotes passed through Camille Laurent's auction house safe! The banknote tracking system confirms this.

But Camille: 'I gave cash tips to more than 50 people on the night of the gala. Those banknotes could be anywhere. I gave them to waiters, drivers, coat check attendants.'

Camille's tip distribution is indeed verified. The banknotes passed through 10+ people's hands. The trail goes cold here.

But the timing of Philippe's confession is critical: he left the corridor empty between 22:30-23:00. EXACTLY the window when the heist took place.

choices.text: Listen to waiter Marc Duval's statement
