scenes.addCharacterNote: Philippe was bribed: 500 Euros, leave the corridor empty for 30 min. Banknotes from Camille's safe BUT passed through 10+ people's hands.

scenes.text:
You enter Antoine Beaumont's home with a search warrant. A luxury apartment in the 16th arrondissement. Walls of the living room are filled with artworks.

But behind the bedroom there's a hidden room. On the wall is a large photograph of Le Dernier Souffle, below it in handwriting: 'YOU WILL BE MINE - 2005'.

Files on the desk: the painting's history, provenance documents, 7 different purchase offers. The last offer: 180 million Euros, rejected by Marguerite 8 months ago.

Also a draft letter: written to Camille, titled 'one last attempt through legal channels'. He asked Camille to research auction channels. Letter date: 3 weeks before the gala.

Plus a map: a copy of the museum floor plan, with the service corridor marked.

But the most shocking find is in the drawer: 8 months of correspondence with Mehmet Kaya, a renowned replica artist in Istanbul. Kaya is known for making 'museum-quality replicas' in the Grand Bazaar. In the correspondence, Antoine sent high-resolution photos of Le Dernier Souffle and requested an 'exact copy'. Kaya's last message: 'The piece is ready, I can come to Paris for delivery in November.' Date: 2 weeks before the gala.

Did Antoine order a replica? For his collection, or for a swap?

choices.text: Question what Antoine was doing on Floor B
Ask Camille about the business partnership with Antoine
Check the museum plan map with JP
Investigate the replica artist in Istanbul

scenes.addCharacterNote: Hidden room in Antoine's home + 8 months of correspondence with Istanbul replica artist Mehmet Kaya! Ordered an exact copy of Le Dernier Souffle, 'delivery to Paris in November', 2 weeks before the gala. A 3rd unfinished Le Dernier Souffle copy?

scenes.text:
You search Camille Laurent's auction house warehouse. The building on the Champs-Elysees, basement floor is the warehouse.

Dozens of crated artworks on the shelves. But one crate catches your eye: painting-sized, empty, shipping address: 'Galerie Volkov, Zurich, Switzerland'.

Dimitri Volkov - Nadia's father's former partner! Nadia's father Dmitri Sokolov and Dimitri Volkov worked together in the 1990s.

Camille: 'Volkov is a legitimate collector. A client of our auction house. This crate was for a Monet last month, it was delivered, the crate was left empty.'

Warehouse records are checked: The Monet delivery is verified. But there's something else in the same warehouse: an insurance inspection form from Isabelle Chen, from 2 days before the gala night. Isabelle inspected this warehouse too.

Even more interesting: the warehouse's security camera shows movement at 02:00 on the night of the gala. But the footage is dark, the person cannot be identified.

choices.text: Talk to Nadia about her father

scenes.addCharacterNote: Zurich shipping crate in Camille's warehouse (Volkov = Nadia's father's partner). Isabelle inspected the warehouse. Unclear figure in the night footage.

scenes.text:
You examine Remi Vasseur's personal records. 6 months ago he went to Auvergne for 2 weeks for a 'family visit'.

But the hotel records in Auvergne: Remi didn't stay there. Where was he?

At an art restoration workshop in Italy. The workshop owner near Florence, Maestro Carlucci: 'Remi came, worked on Caravaggio techniques, very talented. For 2 weeks he was learning pigment analysis, canvas aging, and crack pattern creation techniques.'

This information can be interpreted two ways: Either he was learning techniques for a fake painting, or he was doing practical research for his Sorbonne paper.

From the Sorbonne, Prof. Marchetti (Remi's academic advisor): 'I recommended he attend this workshop. We needed to collect field data for our paper. The Caravaggio collection in Florence was ideal for this.'

BUT Isabelle Chen also attended the same workshop 3 months earlier - 'for insurance evaluation'. Did Remi and Isabelle meet in Italy? Both deny it.

choices.text: Question Remi about the materials in the cabinet
Question the timing of Isabelle's Floor B inspection

scenes.addCharacterNote: Remi said 'family visit' but was at a Caravaggio workshop in Italy. Prof. Marchetti confirms BUT Isabelle also attended the same workshop! Did they meet in Italy?

scenes.text:
Prof. Elena Marchetti from the Sorbonne connects via video conference. In her 60s, cheerful, academic.

'Remi Vasseur is my doctoral student. We've been working on Caravaggio pigment analysis for 2 years. Our paper will be submitted to the Journal of Conservation Science. Remi has a previously published paper in the same journal - verifiable.'

She shows the paper: 'Baroque Period Pigment Degradation and Modern Detection Methods' (Vasseur, R., 2024, JCS Vol.42, pp.187-203). A real journal, a real paper.

'The materials in the workshop were procured on my recommendation. The portion covered by the museum budget is officially under the Sorbonne-Musee des Lumieres cooperation protocol.'

Everything checks out: The protocol is real, registered at the Sorbonne. Prof. Marchetti's academic background is clean.

BUT Marchetti adds something else: 'Remi has changed in recent months. He used to be calm, focused. Lately he's been arriving late to meetings, whispering on the phone. Once he canceled our appointment, wouldn't say why. He just said 'things are complicated at the museum.'

What was complicated at the museum?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Prof. Marchetti confirms Remi's academic work. BUT Remi has changed recently, things were complicated at the museum.

scenes.text:
You examine Nadia Sokolov's portfolio at the Paris School of Fine Arts.

6 different Old Master copies:
1. Vermeer - 'Girl with a Pearl Earring' (perfect copy)
2. Rembrandt - 'The Night Watch' (detail study)
3. Caravaggio - 'Judith and Holofernes' (completed)
4. Titian - 'Bacchus and Ariadne' (unfinished)
5. Velazquez - 'Las Meninas' (detail study)
6. Caravaggio - 'Supper at Emmaus' (perfect copy)

But behind the portfolio, in a separate folder, you find a 7th study: an unfinished copy of Le Dernier Souffle. Same dimensions, same chiaroscuro technique, same pigment mixture. Why did Nadia hide this? Date: started 4 months ago.

Her instructor Prof. Girard: 'Nadia has extraordinary talent. Especially in Caravaggio techniques. Her use of chiaroscuro is nearly at the masters' level. I didn't know about the Le Dernier Souffle study, it must be her personal project.'

The Caravaggio copies in the portfolio are the SAME quality as the technique in the fake painting. And now there's an unfinished copy of Le Dernier Souffle too.

But there's something more: On Nadia's laptop are her father Dmitri's old theft files. 3 museum heists, all using the fake painting swap method. Nadia: 'I'm researching my father's past, I'm ashamed of him, I'm trying to get him treatment.' Her father's Alzheimer's has been confirmed.

Remi's sketch in the workshop cabinet and Nadia's hidden copy, both Le Dernier Souffle, both unfinished. Who copied whose work?

choices.text: Compare the fake painting with Nadia's copies

scenes.addCharacterNote: A HIDDEN 7th study in Nadia's portfolio: an unfinished copy of Le Dernier Souffle! Same painting as the sketch in Remi's cabinet. Both worked on Le Dernier Souffle, who made the fake? Talent + knowledge + opportunity + hidden copy = one of the most concrete pieces of physical evidence.

scenes.text:
Isabelle Chen's financial history is being investigated in depth.

In the Interpol database, Isabelle's name appears on the 'active operations list'. BUT the list's details are classified.

The financial investigation reveals shocking results: Isabelle has an offshore account in the Cayman Islands. 890,000 Euros accumulated over the last 3 years, but 400,000 Euros has been withdrawn in the last 3 months. Where did the money go?

Worse still: Isabelle's insurance company is going through a financial crisis. It reported losses in the last 2 quarters. Isabelle's position is at risk, the company is planning downsizing. If the painting isn't found, the company pays 200 million Euros in damages and Isabelle receives 1.5% commission: 3 million Euros. This would save Isabelle.

Isabelle's official income is 95,000 Euros per year. Where did the 890,000 Euros in savings come from? Where did the 400,000 Euros go?

Isabelle: 'My finances are private. I can't disclose anything related to Interpol operations.'

This answer supports both innocence and guilt.
scenes.addCharacterNote: 400K Euros missing from Isabelle's offshore account. Her company is in financial crisis, her position is at risk. If the painting isn't found, 3M Euro commission = salvation. As desperate as Marguerite!

scenes.text:
You examine the log records of the terminal in the restoration workshop.

The records of the last person to access the terminal on the night of the gala have been DELETED. The deletion was performed at 23:15 - after the heist.

But the source IP of the deletion: the terminal in the security room. Lucien's terminal.

Lucien is in prison. We know his card was cloned. The terminal access could also have been made with the cloned card.

Technician Pierre: 'Terminal access requires a card + password. If the card was cloned, the password must also be known. Only 2 people know the admin password: me and Jean-Pierre.'

But wait: Marguerite as director could request the admin password. Nadia as an intern could have asked the technician for the password. And Hugo spent time in the security room during his interview.

The deletion of the terminal logs is an important lead. Who deleted them and why?

choices.text: Is JP's admin password still valid?
Who did Marguerite share the vault code with?
What did Hugo do in the security room?

scenes.addCharacterNote: Workshop terminal logs were deleted! Deletion was done from the security room terminal. Lucien's card was cloned but the password is also needed. JP, Marguerite, Nadia, or Hugo could have gained access.

scenes.text:
You launch an international investigation into the replica artist Mehmet Kaya in Istanbul.

The Turkish police report arrives a few hours later:

Mehmet Kaya, 62 years old, has been making 'museum-quality replicas' in the Grand Bazaar for 35 years. His official business is legal, producing decorative copies for museums, hotels, and collectors. However, he's on Interpol's watch list: in 2019, his name came up in a fake painting scandal in Vienna, acquitted due to insufficient evidence.

A search of Kaya's workshop found a completed copy of Le Dernier Souffle. Dimensions, pigments, aging technique, when compared to the fake painting at the museum, the quality level is very close.

Kaya's statement: 'Antoine Beaumont contacted me 8 months ago. He wanted an exact copy for his collection. He paid 75,000 Euros. I made it, sent it to Paris at the beginning of November. The DHL tracking number is here.'

The shipping record is verified: The package was delivered to Antoine's address 10 days before the gala.

But the critical question: Was the fake painting at the museum made by Kaya, with the materials in Remi's workshop, or with Nadia's technique? Three different sources, three different Le Dernier Souffle copies. Forensic comparison result: 'Pigment profiles are very similar but an exact match cannot be made. All three copies are of compatible quality with the fake painting at the museum.'
scenes.addCharacterNote: Antoine ordered a Le Dernier Souffle copy from Istanbul! Delivered 10 days before the gala. Now there are 3 different partial/complete copies: Remi's sketch, Nadia's unfinished copy, Antoine's Istanbul order. Which one made the museum fake? Forensics couldn't make a definitive match.

scenes.text:
You've gathered enough information. Now it's time to make an accusation.

Paris Police Headquarters conference room. The prosecutor, lawyers, and investigation team have taken their seats. The 8 suspects and the arrested Lucien sit in a row.

There are 2 accomplices in this case. Who do you accuse?

Be careful: you must choose 2 people. A wrong choice could ruin an innocent person's life.

You examine the security records of people who entered the director's office over the last 6 months.

Entry logs:

1. Isabelle Chen, 4 times. For insurance audits. Stayed 30-45 minutes each time. Did she approach the desk? Interior cameras don't show.

2. Camille Laurent, 2 times. Exhibition planning meetings. Waited alone for 10 minutes during the first visit when Marguerite wasn't there.

3. Hugo Marchand, 1 time. For an interview. Took photos, documents on the desk are visible in the photographs.

4. Antoine Beaumont, 3 times. Painting offer discussions. During his last visit, security personnel noted he 'accidentally' opened the wrong drawer.

The notebook is in the right drawer of the desk. Any of these 4 people could have seen the code. But the most suspicious detail: the drawer Antoine 'accidentally' opened is exactly the drawer where the notebook is.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Office visitors: Isabelle (4 times), Camille (10 min alone), Hugo (photographed the desk), Antoine ('accidentally' opened the notebook drawer). All could have seen the code.

scenes.text:
You listen more deeply to Hugo's theory about the Nazi plunder era.

'In 1943, the Nazis looted dozens of museums in Paris. Le Dernier Souffle disappeared during that period, reappearing in 1962 in a Swiss collection. The museum purchased it in 1975 but the 20 years in between are dark.'

Hugo opens a file: 'The owner of the painting between 1943-1962: Albrecht Kessler, Nazi officer. The Kessler family still lives in Switzerland and wants the painting returned. The lawsuit they filed in 2019 was rejected but the appeal process continues.'

He looks at you: 'Now think, if the painting is stolen, the return lawsuit is dropped. The museum gets the insurance money, the Kessler family loses their right to sue. Who benefits? Museum management.'

This theory creates a new motive for Marguerite: paying off debts + blocking the return lawsuit.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Hugo's theory: the painting has a Nazi-era past, the Kessler family filed a return lawsuit. If the painting is stolen, the lawsuit is dropped and the museum gets insurance. Double motive for Marguerite.

scenes.text:
You look at the investigation board. Photos of 8 suspects, evidence, connecting threads.

Who do you want to interrogate?

- Marguerite Delacroix, Museum director, 3.1 million Euros in debt
- Remi Vasseur, Restorer, 15 years of frustration
- Isabelle Chen, Insurance inspector, black market contacts
- Antoine Beaumont, Collector, 20-year obsession
- Camille Laurent, Auctioneer, encrypted calls
- Nadia Sokolov, Intern, KGB father
- Hugo Marchand, Journalist, suspicious timing
- Jean-Pierre Dumas, Former security architect, revenge motive

You investigate the Isabelle-Jean-Pierre connection.

The photograph taken at the Montmartre cafe: 1 week before the gala, Isabelle and Jean-Pierre sitting side by side. Both had claimed not to know each other.

You dig deeper:

1. Isabelle's insurance company SecureGuard audited Jean-Pierre's former firm SecureArt Paris's security, 2 years ago. There is a business connection.

2. The Turner heist in London: Jean-Pierre's designed security system, Isabelle's audited insurance policy. Same museum, same year.

3. A note in Jean-Pierre's home in the 'November Plan' file: 'Coordinate with I.C.' I.C. = Isabelle Chen?

Are these two appearing in the same cases by coincidence, or is there a systematic partnership? Theory A is strengthening.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Isabelle-JP connection: same London case, secret cafe meeting, 'Coordinate with I.C.' note. Systematic partnership suspicion is strong.

scenes.text:
You investigate the secret dealings of the Antoine-Camille duo.

Encrypted messages decoded:

- 'November package ready', 3 days before the gala, from Camille to Antoine.
- 'Zurich confirmed', 1 day before the gala, from Antoine to Camille.
- 'Buyer is impatient', gala night 23:15, Camille's Zurich call.

The 3rd painting in the Marseille warehouse (no registration number, missing provenance document) has dimensions identical to Le Dernier Souffle. Camille says 'a piece left by a former client' but no client name.

Midnight meeting: Antoine and Camille met at Camille's warehouse 2 nights before the gala. On the security camera, Antoine is carrying a large bag, the same bag found in his hotel room.

The 'buyer' in Zurich is Galerie Volkov, previously investigated for stolen art trade. And Volkov is Nadia's father's former partner.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Antoine-Camille secret operation: encrypted messages, Zurich buyer (Volkov = Nadia's father's partner), unregistered painting in warehouse matching Le Dernier Souffle dimensions, night meeting. Theory B is strengthening.

scenes.text:
You investigate the connection between waiter Marc Duval and Marguerite in depth.

What you find:

1. Duval and Marguerite are members of the same casino: Casino de Monaco, VIP lounge. Both have been regular visitors for the last 2 years.

2. 15,000 Euro cash deposit: The money deposited into Duval's account matches the same day and same amount as cash withdrawn by Marguerite. Marguerite withdrew 15,000 Euros from an ATM that day, her full daily limit.

3. Duval's alibi: He says 'Marguerite sat at the bar all night.' But the bar camera is on a loop, unverifiable.

4. Additional detail: Duval's phone records contain Marguerite's private line. An 8-second call from Duval to Marguerite at 22:55 on the night of the gala. An 'All clear' message?

Did Marguerite pay Duval 15,000 Euros for a fake alibi?
scenes.addCharacterNote: Waiter-Marguerite connection: same casino, 15K Euro cash transfer on the same day, Marguerite's private line in Duval's phone. Marguerite may have bought a fake alibi.

scenes.text:
You send the nighttime footage from Camille's warehouse to the digital forensics team.

Dr. Faure's report: 'We enhanced the footage. A dark figure enters the warehouse at 02:00, exits at 02:23. Spent 23 minutes inside.'

Enhanced details:
- Height: between 170-180cm
- Clothing: dark coat, gloves
- Object carried: rectangular, painting-sized package
- Gait analysis: slight limp, right leg

Is there a suspect with a right leg issue? Antoine has an old skiing injury, right knee. But Remi also sprained his foot in the workshop last year.

The figure wearing gloves indicates professionalism. And 23 minutes is enough time to remove a painting from its crate and place it in another.

No entry card was used for the warehouse, the lock was picked by hand. Locksmith examination: professional lockpick marks.
scenes.addCharacterNote: Warehouse night footage: 170-180cm figure, right leg limp (Antoine skiing injury? Remi sprain?), painting-sized package, 23 min inside, professional lockpick. Brought the fake to the warehouse or took out the original?

endings.title: Flawless Justice
endings.description: Marguerite Delacroix and Remi Vasseur are charged with the Musee des Lumieres heist. All evidence is complete.
endings.epilogueText: Marguerite Delacroix and Remi Vasseur were arrested. Here's the true story: Marguerite had maintained a respected career as museum director for years but was secretly drowning in gambling debt. She owed 2 million Euros to Monaco casinos. Remi Vasseur had worked for years at the museum without recognition, his talents ignored. He wanted to open his own restoration workshop but had no money. Marguerite knew about Remi's dream and brought him a proposal: if they stole the painting, Remi could open his own workshop with his share and prove to those who never appreciated him that they couldn't even tell the difference between his forgery and the real thing. The plan was this: Remi would make a perfect copy of Le Dernier Souffle, and on the night of the gala they would steal the original from the vault. Using Lucien's cloned card, they opened the vault, removed the painting through the service corridor, and replaced it with Remi's fake copy. The coded restoration reports, the technical match in the fake painting, and the hidden service corridor records became irrefutable evidence in court. Le Dernier Souffle was discovered hidden in a safe deposit box in Monaco belonging to Marguerite and was returned to the museum. Lucien Moreau was acquitted of all charges and received compensation from the museum. Marguerite received 15 years, Remi 10 years in prison. Paris's greatest art heist was solved. Bravo, Detective.

endings.title: Great Work, Detective!
endings.description: You found the right people and built a strong case!
endings.epilogueText: Marguerite and Remi were arrested. Marguerite received 8 years, Remi 5 years in prison. Superb work, Detective! Here's the true story: Marguerite was drowning in gambling debt, she owed 2 million Euros to Monaco casinos. Remi had worked for years at the museum without recognition, dreaming of opening his own workshop. Marguerite exploited Remi's dream: if they stole the painting, Remi could open his own workshop with his share and prove that nobody could tell the difference with his forgery. Remi made a perfect copy, on the night of the gala they opened the vault with Lucien's cloned card and removed the original through the service corridor, replacing it with the fake. If you had gathered a few more critical pieces of evidence, the sentences would have been much heavier and the painting could have been found too. But you accomplished the most important thing: innocent Lucien was saved, the real criminals were caught!

endings.title: Weak Case
endings.description: You found the right names but the evidence is insufficient.
endings.epilogueText: Marguerite and Remi were taken into custody but their lawyers demonstrated the insufficiency of evidence. Both were sentenced to house arrest and freed within 2 years. The painting is still missing. Lucien's case also remained uncertain. You know the truth but couldn't prove it.

endings.title: Case Unsolved
endings.description: You accused a single suspect. The evidence chain was incomplete and both walked free.
endings.epilogueText:
The court's verdict is final: you accused a single suspect, but the theft of Le Dernier Souffle clearly required teamwork. A lone thief producing such a flawless forgery, opening the biometric vault and looping the cameras is technically impossible.

Defense lawyers tore that logic gap apart. The prosecution said 'at least two people' but you offered only one name. The jury was not convinced. The accused walked. The real accomplice was never named.

Paris newspapers ran the headline the next morning: 'Musée des Lumières Heist: Insufficient Evidence, No Perpetrator Found'. Lucien Moreau remains in his cell. Le Dernier Souffle is still missing.

endings.title: Innocent Convict
endings.description: You accused Lucien Moreau. He is innocent.
endings.epilogueText: Lucien Moreau received 12 years in prison. The cloned card and fake fingerprint evidence were never raised in court. The real criminals, Marguerite and Remi, sold the painting and disappeared with new identities. Years later, when a newspaper investigation revealed the truth, Lucien had already spent 7 years in prison. A grave miscarriage of justice, Detective.

endings.title: The Collector's Curse
endings.description: You accused Antoine Beaumont. He had an obsession but wasn't the thief.
endings.epilogueText: Antoine Beaumont was taken into custody but his lawyers proved he had no means of accessing the vault. Antoine had been trying to BUY the painting for 20 years, not steal it. The charges were dropped, Antoine filed a compensation lawsuit against the museum. The real criminals remained free.

endings.title: Wrong Inspector
endings.description: You accused Isabelle Chen. She had suspicious connections but wasn't involved in the heist.
endings.epilogueText: Isabelle Chen was taken into custody. Her contact with black market dealer Petrov was investigated but the truth that emerged was different: Isabelle was using Petrov as an informant in another art theft case. You ruined a secret Interpol operation, Detective.

endings.title: Bitter Revenge
endings.description: You accused Jean-Pierre Dumas. He had the knowledge but didn't commit a crime.
endings.epilogueText: Jean-Pierre was arrested. The documents in his home raised suspicion but Jean-Pierre's purpose was different: he was documenting the museum's security vulnerabilities to leak them to the media, as revenge for being fired. He wasn't a thief, just an angry former employee.

endings.title: The Intern's Innocence
endings.description: You accused Nadia Sokolov. She had the father connection but wasn't involved in the heist.
endings.epilogueText: Nadia was taken into custody. Her Moscow trips and father connection were investigated. The truth: Nadia is ashamed of her father Dmitri's past and was trying to get him treatment. The photographs were for showing doctors. You accused the wrong person.

endings.title: Auctioneer Acquitted
endings.description: You accused Camille Laurent. She had black market connections but had nothing to do with this heist.
endings.epilogueText: Camille was taken into custody. The calls on the encrypted phone were examined: the Zurich, Dubai, and Hong Kong contacts were legitimate auction buyers. Camille's auction house truly operated internationally. Everything that looked suspicious was legitimate business activity.

endings.title: Press Freedom
endings.description: You accused Hugo Marchand. His timing was suspicious but he was just a journalist.
endings.epilogueText: Hugo was arrested. Press freedom organizations took to the streets. Hugo's research wasn't aimed at discovering the heist but at questioning the painting's authenticity. Being the first to break the story about the heist was journalistic instinct, not criminal partnership.

There are other suspects besides Lucien.

We have a prime suspect but I can't reveal yet.

OK, I'm on duty. Call me if you need anything.
Lucien, be extra careful tonight. VIP guests.
Understood, Madam. Everything is under control.
Lucien, I left your things at reception.

newspaperArticles.headline: SHOCKING HEIST AT MUSÉE DES LUMIÈRES
newspaperArticles.body: An incredible heist occurred at Paris's most prestigious museum. During the Nuit d'Or gala, Caravaggio's Le Dernier Souffle was stolen from the vault and replaced with a perfect forgery. Night security chief Lucien Moreau was arrested.

newspaperArticles.headline: HEIST INVESTIGATION DEEPENS
newspaperArticles.body: The Musée des Lumières heist investigation is expanding. Beyond the arrested Lucien Moreau, 8 more individuals are being questioned. The lead detective is following the trail.

newspaperArticles.headline: FORGERY OF INCREDIBLE QUALITY
newspaperArticles.subheadline: Experts: Only 10 people in the world could make this
newspaperArticles.body: Analysis of the forgery in the vault is complete. Experts confirm the fake is of 'incredible quality,' made using 17th-century techniques, and took at least 6 months to prepare.

newspaperArticles.headline: DETECTIVE MASTERS THE CASE
newspaperArticles.body: The detective investigating the Musée des Lumières heist has made significant progress. According to police sources, charges are imminent.

newspaperArticles.headline: HAS THE INVESTIGATION HIT A DEAD END?
newspaperArticles.body: No progress in the Musée des Lumières heist. Lawyers and media are criticizing the investigation. Lucien Moreau's lawyer says 'my client is innocent.'

evidenceReactions.note: Marguerite trying to normalize the reports.

evidenceReactions.dialogue: Marguerite purses her lips. 'Vault code? As director, of course I know it. That's normal.'

Jean-Pierre's eyes light up. 'Fake? This scandal invalidates all insurance policies!'
Camille smiles slightly. 'The fake art market is a billion-dollar industry. Many collectors don't even know.'

evidenceReactions.note: Antoine reacted emotionally to the fake painting news.

Isabelle adjusts her glasses. 'A fake painting is a disaster for insurance. The policy could be void.'
Philippe starts sweating. 'That night I was very tired. Maybe my attention slipped for a moment.'
